svg.text: Our design, end to end — and the conventions we're inventing instead of inheriting. React to this; once it's right, it cascades into the docs.
proposal v0.2 · based on docs/plan + design-1
decisions A–E flagged in clay → your call

g.text: ① WHERE genie LIVES — INSIDE YOUR HARNESS, NOT A DESTINATION
Claude Design is a place you open. genie is a teammate that shows up wherever you already work.
genie never builds a chat panel.
your work, made legible to an LLM
the clay accent lives only here
The wedge: genie owns the browser + preview; the harness owns the chat. That seam is the thing no canvas-first competitor can copy.
② TWO THINGS genie OPERATES ON — KITS, AND PROJECTS THAT USE THEM
A kit is the library. A project is the web / app / mobile screens you build with that kit.
Button · Card · Input · tokens · the design language
produces → a card in the grid · the unit of generation today
landing pages, flows, full screens — built from kit parts
produces → a full-page preview · constrained to the bound kit
→ M1 project foundation — seam designed now
WHICH KIT? — genie resolves a default, and flags if it can't
you named a kit in the request → use it
the project's bound default (.genie/project.json) → use it
exactly one kit reachable → use it, name which
→ stop and ask — never guess, never
fall back to generic AI components
"No kit bound to marketing-site. Bind one, or pick: acme-ui, icons-kit."
③ THE ARCHITECTURE — ONE BINARY, EVERYTHING ELSE SWAPPABLE
genie is thin on purpose. It owns the protocol and the preview; storage, models, and auth are yours.
a kit/project is a git-tracked tree (see ④)
you own the model, the budget, the rate limits
No database for content — the git log IS the audit log. sqlite is used only for throwaway server scratch (in-flight plan TTLs). genie ships no model, no storage, no lock-in.
④ WHERE YOUR WORK LIVES — A TREE genie IS JAILED TO, NEVER A REPO-IN-A-REPO
A kit/project is its own repo, or a subtree of yours, or a plain folder. genie writes only inside that root.
two repos, separate dirs. They meet at
no second repo. genie writes files;
just a directory genie writes to.
git init it for history — or don't.
WHEN genie OWNS THE REPO (shape A) — the verbs map straight onto git
As a guest in your repo (B), this degrades gracefully: plan still scopes & validates the write — but the commit is yours, no PRs opened behind your back.
⑤ THE CORE LOOP — DESCRIBE → CONJURE → PREVIEW → REFINE → COMMIT
The whole product is one tight loop. The clay accent marks the two moments that are genie's magic.
lands in your tree as a

The clay accent appears on Conjure and Refine only — the design system's one rule. Structure stays ink; generation glows clay. (Projects run the same loop, one step up: a screen instead of a component.)
⑥ genie's OWN PROTOCOL — DECISION A (your call)
Same shape as DesignSync — read freely, ask once, write scoped — but our own names.
verb-first reads better than get_; list_files kept
THE GATE — the one permission boundary
approve a scoped set of write globs → planId
WRITE — only within the granted plan
the marker IS the registration (panel ⑦)
returns the ui:// grid for MCP-App hosts

Kit core: 16 verbs → 13. Drop 2 (register/unregister), merge 2 (the validators). The namespace mcp__genie__* is what every harness sees. (M1 also ships project verbs — list_projects/bind_kit/conjure_screen.)
⑦ genie's OWN CONVENTIONS — DECISIONS B · C · D · E
Four more inherited details, four fresh choices. Each is a proposal — change any of them.
first line still registers the card — just our name
our improvement: one tidy dir, not files at the root
compiled from markers; the viewer reads it
MCP-App stays ui://genie/grid — that prefix is spec-fixed
⑧ INSIDE THE TWO CLAY MOMENTS — GROUNDING IN, VALIDATION OUT
The model writes the code. genie's job is to make it provably yours — that conjunction is the moat, not the LLM.
your tokens · existing primitives' .d.ts contracts · adherence rules · few-shot examples
inject grounding + the 5-file artifact contract + marker requirement + framework
Constrained generation — the one model call
your endpoint · response_format: json_schema forces 5 well-formed files · streamed
The validation gauntlet — this is where "yours" is enforced
any check fails → one self-repair retry, feeding the validator's error back to the model
ts-morph reads the .tsx → emits .d.ts — the grounding the next generation reads
plan → write → anchor — it lands as a real, validated git commit
REFINE — mutate one thing, provably freeze the rest
current files + instruction → the model returns a unified diff, applied with patch,
then re-validated through the same gauntlet.
asking for a diff is what makes "the rest is untouched" provable — it can't restate what it didn't change
at generation, genie detects the component's axes — size, radius, shadow, accent —
and surfaces them as knobs mapped to token values.
dragging a slider makes no model call — instant & free. only a structural change hits the LLM
a comment-pin gives a rect; genie annotates the prompt with it
("limit changes to this region") and scopes the diff where it can.
mapping a pixel rect → source lines is the hard part — in v1 it's a hint, not a hard constraint
THE HONEST LINE — WHAT genie CAN AND CANNOT VERIFY

genie mechanically verifies "it's your code" — imports tokens, no raw hex, passes adherence, renders. It cannot verify "it's beautiful." Taste scales with whatever model you point it at; genie is the harness, not the brain.
⑨ WHAT genie IS NOT — AND HOW INTEROP FITS
✕ a hosted SaaS ✕ a drag-to-reflow canvas ✕ a Figma rival
✕ a token/color tool ✕ a content database ✕ our own LLM ✕ locked file formats
INTEROP IS A FUTURE BRIDGE, NOT THE FOUNDATION
genie speaks its own conventions natively. An opt-in mode can later
read/write @dsCard to round-trip Claude Design — and import Google Stitch.

svg.text: genie · explainer · how genie works · a proposal to react to · decisions A–E in clay → own conventions first, bridges later
